reasoning: BUTTERFLY is a classic example of a phased sortal. It represents a temporal phase in the life cycle of a lepidopteran insect. An individual butterfly was previously a caterpillar and is the same organism throughout, but it is not *necessarily* a butterfly at all times of its existence - hence ~R (anti-rigid). It carries identity (+I) inherited from its subsuming type LEPIDOPTERAN, which supplies the global identity condition that allows us to track the same individual across metamorphosis. However, BUTTERFLY itself does not supply its own global identity condition (-O), only a local one valid during the butterfly phase. As a biological organism in a specific phase, instances are intrinsic wholes (+U). Butterflies are not existentially dependent on external entities beyond normal biological requirements (-D). The paper explicitly uses the caterpillar/butterfly example to illustrate phased sortals: 'an individual may at one time be a CATERPILLAR and at another time be a BUTTERFLY... it must be possible to determine that they are the same entity at these two times' through subsumption under LEPIDOPTERAN.

RED is a color property that represents an attribution. It is anti-rigid (~R) because entities can change color - something can be red at one time and not red at another while remaining the same entity. It does not carry identity (-I, -O) because knowing something is red does not provide conditions for distinguishing one red thing from another or re-identifying it over time. It lacks unity criteria (-U) as it is an attribute that can be predicated of various kinds of things without imposing whole-part structure. It is dependent (+D) because redness cannot exist independently - it must inhere in some entity (an object must be red; redness requires a bearer). This fits the framework's example of attributions as 'non-sortal properties that are non-rigid, normally anti-rigid, representing values of attributes like COLOR-OF' with RED explicitly mentioned as an example attribution.

FOOD is anti-rigid (~R) because something is food only when it serves the functional role of being consumed for nutrition - an apple can cease being food after being eaten or rotting, yet remains the same object. It carries identity (+I) because it must be subsumed by types like FRUIT, VEGETABLE, or MEAT that supply identity conditions. It does not supply its own identity (-O) since the same physical object (e.g., an apple) has its identity determined by what it fundamentally is (a fruit), not by its role as food. Unity is non-unifying (-U) because food can be wholes (an apple) or non-wholes (a portion of rice, a pile of beans). It is dependent (+D) because food exists in relation to consumers - something is food only in the context of being edible/consumable by some organism. This matches the paper's explicit classification of FOOD as a material role that 'specializes formal roles and has specific meaning in a domain.'

ANIMAL is a rigid property (+R) because something that is an animal is necessarily an animal in all possible worlds - an entity cannot cease to be an animal while remaining the same entity. It carries identity (+I) and supplies its own identity condition (+O) - animals have clear biological identity criteria (same genetic makeup, continuous biological organism). Animals possess unity (+U) as they are integrated biological wholes with functional organization and topological connectedness. They are independent (-D) as they do not require external entities beyond their parts to exist. As a rigid property that supplies its own identity (+O+R), ANIMAL classifies as a Type - one of the fundamental sortals that organizes the domain and provides identity criteria for its instances.

RedApple is anti-rigid (~R) because an apple can change color (from green to red, or red to brown) while remaining the same apple - being red is not essential to the apple's identity across all times. It carries identity (+I) inherited from APPLE, which supplies the identity condition for what makes something the same apple over time. It does not supply its own identity (-O) since the identity comes from APPLE as a type. RedApple has unity (+U) as each red apple is an intrinsic whole with clear boundaries. It is independent (-D) as a red apple does not require external entities for its existence beyond normal physical dependencies. This represents a phased sortal - a temporal phase in an apple's lifecycle where it exhibits the property of being red, similar to how CATERPILLAR and BUTTERFLY are phases of LEPIDOPTERAN in the paper's example.

GROUP is rigid (+R) because an entity that is a group is necessarily a group in all possible worlds - it cannot cease being a group while remaining the same entity. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O): groups have identity criteria based on their membership constitution and organizational structure (e.g., 'having the same members under the same organizing principle'). GROUP exhibits unity (+U) as instances are intrinsic wholes - a group is unified by the organizational/social relations binding its members together, not merely an arbitrary collection. It is independent (-D) as groups can exist without being parts of other entities. Following the meta-property combinations, GROUP qualifies as a Type: a rigid property that supplies its own identity condition, representing a fundamental kind in the domain of collective entities.

Based on the paper's own analysis in the taxonomy cleaning example, COUNTRY (when properly disambiguated from geographic region) is treated as a rigid property that supplies its own identity condition. The paper states that 'COUNTRY is classified under SOCIAL ENTITY and LEGAL ENTITY' after splitting it from GEOGRAPHICAL REGION to resolve the initial inconsistency between +O and ~R. As a social-legal entity, a country maintains its identity through continuity of governance/sovereignty (not mere geographic boundaries). It carries +U as countries are unified wholes (not mere aggregates of land). Countries exist independently (-D) as sovereign entities. The combination +R+I+O-D classifies COUNTRY as a Type - the most fundamental kind of property that supplies identity to its instances.

AmountOfMatter is rigid (+R) because something that is an amount of matter is necessarily so in all possible worlds - it cannot cease to be an amount of matter while remaining the same entity. It carries and supplies its own identity (+I, +O) through mereological extensionality: two amounts of matter are the same iff they have the same parts (this is explicitly mentioned in the paper as an identity criterion for amounts of matter). It has anti-unity (~U) because amounts of matter are arbitrary mereological sums without intrinsic wholeness - they have no unifying relation binding their parts together (the paper explicitly states 'we assume that none of them has a unity condition, thus ~U'). It is independent (-D) as amounts of matter do not require external entities to exist. The combination +O, +I, +R, -D classifies it as a Type according to Table 3.

PhysicalObject is a rigid property (+R) because anything that is a physical object is necessarily so in all possible worlds - if something exists as a physical object, it cannot cease to be a physical object while remaining the same entity. It supplies its own identity condition (+O, which implies +I) based on spatiotemporal continuity and material constitution - physical objects can be reidentified through space and time. The paper explicitly describes physical objects as 'isolated material entities' with unity (+U), meaning each instance constitutes an intrinsic whole with parts bound together (e.g., an apple is a unified physical object, but an arbitrary undetached part is not considered a separate physical object). Physical objects are independent (-D) as they don't require external entities beyond their parts to exist. As a rigid property supplying its own identity (+O +R), PhysicalObject qualifies as a Type - one of the fundamental categories in ontology that supplies identity conditions and forms part of the backbone taxonomy.

GroupOfPeople is rigid (+R) because any instance that is a group of people remains necessarily a group of people across all possible worlds - the group cannot cease to be a group of people while remaining the same entity. It supplies its own identity condition (+O, therefore +I) based on which specific people constitute the group at a given time - two groups are the same iff they are composed of the same members. However, it does not carry a common unity condition (-U) because different groups may be unified by different relations (e.g., a family vs. a committee vs. a crowd - each has different unifying principles, or some groups may be arbitrary collections). It is independent (-D) as groups of people do not intrinsically depend on external entities beyond their members (who are parts/constituents, not external). As a rigid property supplying its own IC, GroupOfPeople qualifies as a Type in the ontological framework.

LivingBeing is a rigid property (+R) because an entity that is a living being is necessarily so in all possible worlds where it exists - nothing can stop being a living being and remain the same entity (when it dies, it ceases to exist as that entity). It supplies its own identity condition (+O, thus +I) based on biological continuity and life processes - what makes the same living being the same across time is tied to continuous biological functioning and metabolic processes. It carries unity (+U) as living beings are paradigmatic examples of intrinsic wholes with biological/functional unity - their parts are bound together by biological organization and life processes. It is independent (-D) as living beings exist in their own right, not essentially dependent on other external entities. This combination (+O, +I, +R, -D) classifies LivingBeing as a Type - a fundamental category that supplies identity and serves as a backbone sortal in ontologies, as suggested in the paper's example taxonomies where LIVING-BEING appears as a key organizing concept.

SOCIAL ENTITY is analyzed as a rigid property (+R) because entities like organizations, communities, or nations remain social entities throughout their existence - they cannot cease being social entities while continuing to exist as the same entity. It carries identity (+I) and supplies its own identity condition (+O) - social entities have distinct identity criteria based on their social structure, membership rules, and institutional frameworks that distinguish one social entity from another (e.g., what makes Organization A different from Organization B). The property exhibits unity (+U) as social entities are conceived as pluralities with 'social unity' - they are wholes bound together by social relationships and structures, not mere aggregates. While social entities depend on their members to exist, this is better understood as a constitution relation rather than external dependence, so -D is assigned. The combination of +R, +O (which implies +I), and the ability to supply identity criteria for collective social entities qualifies SOCIAL ENTITY as a Type in the formal ontology framework.

Location is a rigid property that applies necessarily to its instances (a spatial or temporal region cannot cease to be a location while remaining the same entity). However, it does not carry identity conditions - there is no clear criterion for when two locations are the same location across all contexts (locations can be spatial regions, temporal regions, connected or disconnected). The paper explicitly mentions Location as an example of a Category in the taxonomy cleaning example, noting locations can be 'spatial or temporal regions' and 'can be either connected or not', thus no unity condition (-U). Locations are ontologically independent entities (-D). As a Category, it is rigid but lacks identity conditions, serving as a high-level organizing principle that 'carves the domain into useful segments.'

FRUIT is a rigid property (+R) because once something is a fruit (the mature ovary of a flowering plant), it remains essentially a fruit throughout its existence - an apple cannot cease to be a fruit while continuing to exist as the same entity. It carries and supplies its own identity condition (+I, +O) based on botanical origin and developmental process (the transformation of a flower's ovary after fertilization). Each fruit instance is an intrinsic whole (+U) with topological and morphological unity - it has definite boundaries and unified structure. FRUIT is independent (-D) as fruits do not require external entities beyond their own constitution to exist (though they historically depended on a plant, once formed they exist independently). As a rigid property supplying its own identity, FRUIT qualifies as a Type - a fundamental natural kind in biological taxonomy that provides identity conditions for its instances.

VERTEBRATE is rigid (+R) because any vertebrate is necessarily a vertebrate in all possible worlds - an entity cannot cease being a vertebrate while continuing to exist. It carries identity (+I) as it is subsumed by biological types like ANIMAL that supply identity conditions. However, it does not supply its own identity (-O) but rather inherits it from its superordinate type (likely ANIMAL or LIVING BEING). The paper explicitly mentions 'VERTEBRATE-ANIMAL' as an example of a quasi-type. Vertebrates have unity (+U) as they are biological wholes with integrated parts bound by biological organization. They are independent (-D) as they don't require external entities to exist beyond their constituent parts. As a rigid sortal that carries but does not supply identity, VERTEBRATE is classified as a Quasi-Type - it introduces necessary biological membership conditions (having a backbone/vertebral column) but inherits its identity criteria from more general biological types.

CATERPILLAR is a classic phased sortal. It is anti-rigid (~R) because an entity that is a caterpillar does not remain one necessarily—it becomes a butterfly while remaining the same individual lepidopteran. It carries identity (+I) inherited from its subsuming type (LEPIDOPTERAN), but does not supply its own global identity condition (-O), only a local IC for that temporal phase. A caterpillar is an intrinsic whole (+U) as a unified biological organism. It is independent (-D) as it exists as a complete entity without depending on another distinct entity for its existence (though biologically it may need food, this is not the ontological dependence meant here). As noted in the paper's example: 'an individual may at one time be a CATERPILLAR and at another time be a BUTTERFLY... it is still the same entity according to the global ICs' supplied by LEPIDOPTERAN.

PERSON is a paradigmatic example of a Type. It is rigid (+R) because if x is a person, x must be a person in every possible world where x exists - one cannot cease being a person while remaining the same individual. It supplies its own identity condition (+O), such as 'having the same fingerprints' or 'having the same SSN' as mentioned in the paper, making it also carry identity (+I). Persons are intrinsic wholes (+U) with clear boundaries distinguishing their parts from the rest of the world through biological/functional unity. Persons are independent (-D) as they do not require external entities (beyond their parts/constituents) to exist. As a Type, PERSON represents a fundamental sortal that supplies identity and serves as a backbone of the taxonomy, satisfying the Sortal Individuation principle.

LegalAgent is a role property that entities acquire when involved in legal contracts or relationships. It is anti-rigid (~R) because entities are not necessarily legal agents in all possible worlds - a person becomes a legal agent only when participating in legal activities, and can cease to be one. It does not carry identity (-I, -O) because legal agents can be persons, organizations, or countries, each with different identity conditions - there is no single identity criterion for all legal agents. It lacks unity (-U) as its instances can be diverse wholes (individual persons, corporate bodies) without a common unifying relation. It is dependent (+D) because being a legal agent requires participation in legal relationships or contracts - one cannot be a legal agent in isolation. As an anti-rigid, dependent, non-sortal property, it classifies as a Formal Role, representing a relational status that entities can acquire and lose while maintaining their identity.

Entity is the most universal property in ontology, representing anything that can exist. It is +R (rigid) because everything that is an entity is necessarily so across all possible worlds - nothing can cease being an entity while continuing to exist. It is -I because Entity itself does not carry any identity condition - it is too general to supply criteria for distinguishing one entity from another. Consequently, it is -O (does not supply its own identity). It is -U because entities can be wholes, parts, or collections with no unified constraint. It is -D because the concept of entity does not depend on any other property - it is the most fundamental category. As a rigid property without identity (+R, -I, -O), Entity classifies as a Category - the highest-level rigid property that carves the domain but provides no identity criteria. Categories are 'properties that are rigid but do not carry identity' and are 'normally the highest level rigid properties in an ontology.'

ORGANIZATION is rigid (+R) because an organization necessarily remains an organization throughout its existence - it cannot cease being an organization while continuing to exist as the same entity. It supplies its own identity condition (+O, thus +I) since organizations have specific identity criteria (e.g., legal registration, continuity of purpose and structure) that distinguish one organization from another, even when membership changes. The paper explicitly notes that 'the same group of people can make different organizations,' indicating organizations are more than their constituent members and have distinct identity conditions. Organizations carry unity (+U) as they are intrinsic wholes bound together by organizational structure, governance, and purpose - they are not mere aggregates of people. Finally, organizations are independent (-D) as they exist as autonomous entities, not intrinsically dependent on any external entity for their existence (though they depend on having members, these are parts/constituents, not external dependencies). As a rigid property supplying its own identity (+O+R), ORGANIZATION qualifies as a Type - a fundamental kind in the ontology.

Agent is anti-rigid (~R) because being an agent is contingent—an entity is only an agent while engaged in action or agency relationships, not necessarily so across all possible worlds. As stated in the paper's example, 'something is an agent only while it is involved in an action.' It does not carry identity (-I, -O) because agents can be persons, organizations, software systems, etc., each with different identity conditions—there's no common identity criterion for all agents. Agent lacks unity (-U) as its instances (individual persons, multi-person organizations) don't share a single unity condition. It is dependent (+D) because agency is relational—being an agent requires the existence of actions, patients, or contexts external to the agent itself. These characteristics (anti-rigid, non-sortal, dependent) classify Agent as a Formal Role—a top-level role property that cannot be subsumed by sortals and serves to organize role taxonomies.

APPLE is a natural kind term referring to a fruit. It is rigid (+R) because anything that is an apple is necessarily an apple in all possible worlds where it exists - an apple cannot cease being an apple while remaining the same entity. It supplies its own identity condition (+O, which implies +I) - apples have clear criteria for being the same apple over time (spatiotemporal continuity, biological development from the same organism). It carries unity (+U) as each apple is an intrinsic whole with functional and topological unity - all parts are bound together as a coherent biological entity. It is independent (-D) as apples do not require existential dependence on other entities beyond their constitutive parts (they needed to grow from a tree, but once existing, they are independent entities). As a rigid property that supplies its own identity (+O +R), APPLE is classified as a Type - the most fundamental category for sortals in the ontological framework.
